data.title: International Consolidated Airlines: Deep Value While Everyone Watches The War
data.description:
Introduction

International Consolidated Airlines Group S.A. (OTCPK:ICAGY) (OTCPK:BABWF) (BMAD:IAG) (LSE:IAG) is not a well-known name in the U.S. investing circle, and most likely, this is the reason for this stock to be so undervalued. It’s an OTC-traded European airline group that owns British Airways, Iberia, Aer Lingus, and Vueling. The stock price has increased by about 100% in the past year. It has strong fundamentals and a surprisingly resilient Q1 performance. And I have to say, the setup looks like it’s just the beginning. This stock dipped today almost 5% on the Middle East news, and as an Israeli, I have to say, I see here the potential for a very quick gain, and I will make this trade.

I’m writing this while sitting in Israel, and honestly, having no idea what happens next. This is what it looks like when macro risk becomes a reality. The only thing I know for sure, everything passes, especially when the news is not so fresh, and in my opinion, by Monday, the market will start bouncing back slowly (if things don’t get out of perspective, of course, but it doesn’t look like it). Besides, conflicts like this typically send airline stocks down, but not all of them deserve to be treated the same way. IAG has something most airlines don’t have. They earn nice money, and this stock, even after rallying 100%, trades at a deep-value multiple. Stocks like this have no business crashing on the news, but the market is moved by the sentiment, so all we have left to do is make money on it.

Company Overview & Q1 Results

The company posted a Q1 operating profit of €198 million, up €130 million year-over-year. Despite disruptions like Heathrow’s one-day closure (which cost British Airways €50M), late Easter timing, and FX headwinds, IAG improved operating margin by 1.7 points to 2.8%.

Revenue rose 9.6% year-over-year to €7 billion, driven by robust transatlantic and Latin American demand. Iberia and British Airways led the profitability gains, with Iberia hitting a 7.5% margin thanks to North and South Atlantic route performance. Vueling (IAG’s low-cost Spanish airline that covers mostly short-distance flights across Europe) saw a slight dip this quarter, but that’s more about the Easter calendar shift than anything else. It’s the most seasonal business in the group, so temporary drops like this aren’t unusual.

According to management, corporate travel is holding up well across all regions, with no major changes so far despite everything going on in the world. And when it comes to passenger demand, the premium cabins are being strong. There’s been some softness in economy bookings from U.S. travelers, which might be due to the macro pressure or just people pulling back a little, but that weakness is being balanced out by strong demand for business and first-class seats.

If we look at the performance by region, the North Atlantic saw the strongest increase in unit revenue of any region year-on-year. Robust performance was evidence across each of the transatlantic airlines, all of them experiencing high single digit or double digit percentage increases in revenue. In particular, premium cabins performed well.

What’s Going On Politically

Right now, geopolitical risk is front and center. Oil is spiking after Israel launched Operation Am Kelevi (Lion Nation), a direct military campaign against Iran’s nuclear infrastructure. That’s obviously not good news for airlines, especially with jet fuel already expected to cost €7.5 billion for IAG this year.

But the funniest thing is that IAG isn’t heavily exposed to the Middle East like the Emirates or Qatar. Of course, it has some exposure, but its core earnings come from transatlantic and European markets, which did not change. Besides, the company has already hedged 65% of its fuel needs for 2025, so the recent spike in oil prices (CL1:COM) isn’t a disaster scenario for them, either. It’ll hurt a bit, sure, but it’s manageable. At the same time, net debt is falling (now down to €6.1 billion), and leverage is already below 1x. In my opinion, this is much more important for this specific stock, than the conflict and the oil prices.

Valuation

ICAGY trades at just 6.41x forward GAAP earnings and EV/EBITDA is only 3.56x. That’s 60–70% lower than sector medians. The group is profitable, with a net income margin close to 9% and a return on equity north of 60%. Is it only me, or is it a running joke that airlines are seldom profitable? From what I always knew about airlines, they are saying that things will turn around this year, and then they will borrow more and more money. This company is not only profitable, but they have growing margins. Totally untypical for this sector.

Risks & Catalysts

The obvious thing I must mention – this is an airline stock. That means it’s never going to be immune to macro pressures, and right now, we have tons of them. The biggest immediate risk is geopolitical. I’m writing this from Israel, where the country just launched a military operation against Iran. Oil spiked over 13% in a day, the biggest jump since Russia invaded Ukraine. And while IAG is well-hedged, as I explained before, it still expects to spend around €7.5 billion on jet fuel this year. If oil stays elevated, or worse, if Iran retaliates in a way that disrupts global supply, fuel costs could pressure margins even with hedging. JPM had actually said that this attack could be the cause of an oil price surge to $120, which sounds exaggerated in my opinion, but who am I to question their prediction?

There’s also the Boeing tragedy. A 787-8 operated by Air India crashed this week, and while IAG’s aircraft weren’t involved, they do fly 787s, and just ordered 71 new wide bodies, many of them from Boeing. That kind of headline doesn’t help investor confidence, especially with safety investigations now underway and Indian regulators reportedly grounding part of the Dreamliner fleet.

Beyond that, demand trends aren’t perfect. Expensive, premium cabins are helping the company right now, but there has been some softness in U.S. economy bookings, which is hard to ignore. Unclear where things will go from here, but if macro stays weak, or if consumers pause traveling, it could definitely hit this company.

That said, there are also some real positives here. The company has already kicked off a major buyback program, which shows confidence from management and usually sends a strong signal to the market. Net debt is going down, margins are moving in the right direction, and they’re still sticking to their plan to grow capacity by around 3% this year, which is ambitious considering the macro backdrop. If things settle down even a little, whether it’s oil prices, market volatility, or geopolitical fears, there’s a good chance ICAGY won’t stay this cheap for long.

And I think it will all calm down sooner than everyone expects. Oil isn’t seeing the biggest demand, and it’s weakening as time passes. OPEC is holding the prices, but the organization has its own difficulties, and if it lets the price go, I can only imagine how quickly it will drop. As for the conflict, I am not a war correspondent, but I have been following the situation here, in Israel, closely enough to understand what is happening.

After today’s attack, it will be difficult for Iran to answer. While we are not aware of the full force of the Iranian military, we know for sure that it was seriously damaged today. As we saw after the Hezbollah and Hamas attacks, people, and commanders are sometimes even more important than the weapons, and when they get unexpectedly taken out, it lowers the chances for successful retribution.

Conclusion

There’s nothing speculative about ICAGY at this point. The company is profitable, actively paying down debt, and growing margins, yet the stock still trades like it’s stuck in the post-COVID meltdown. And yes, the headlines right now are scary about war in the Middle East, oil surging, etc. Investor’s nerves are understandably on edge, it hasn’t been a calm couple of months. But this is exactly the kind of situation where certain stocks get mispriced, not because of their fundamentals, but because they happen to be in the wrong sector at the wrong time. IAG isn’t some fragile budget airline barely hanging on.

International Consolidated Airlines Group S.A. is a well-run, asset-rich group that’s executing on its strategy and returning cash to shareholders. If the market keeps panicking, I’d honestly see that as an opportunity because ICAGY is set to make an epic comeback story that most people haven’t even noticed.

data.title: Tesla's Last Dance: Slumping Deliveries And Political Feud (Downgrade)
data.description:
Last time I wrote about Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) a month ago, and gave it a “Hold.” Since then, the stock has declined nearly 6% and is down 19% year-to-date due to multiple reasons, including the May sales decline, Goldman Sachs' cut in forecast, and the ongoing conflict between President Trump and CEO Elon Musk. Last time, I said that Tesla stock was gaining only on the heels of Trump and Musk's friendship, as well as Trump's policies favoring Musk’s EV company. As we’ve seen since the election, Tesla stock reached new highs in December 2024, following Trump's win.

But now this relationship is fading as Musk’s harsh public criticism of Trump’s "One Big Beautiful Bill," including calls for impeachment and revealing potentially damning information, has triggered a public feud. However, Musk's recent apology eased some worries, but investors are keeping an eye on the upcoming news.

Beyond political worries, Tesla has its own problems. Now, the reality is that Tesla is feeling sales and delivery slumps on the ground. Goldman Sachs just cut Tesla’s targets, noting U.S. deliveries off mid-teens and Europe down roughly 50%. Goldman now projects Q2 2025 deliveries at just 365,000 units, down from its prior estimate of 410,000 and well below the consensus forecast of 417,000. Sales in the month of May 2025 plunged in major markets (e.g., Germany 36%, UK 45% year-over-year).

In short, demand is weak and competition fierce, despite occasional offers or model refreshes.

The only near-term positives are the planned rollout of robotaxis and updates to the Optimus robot. However, these are still “years away” bets; Musk himself says autonomy/Optimus are long-term drivers. Moreover, Tesla’s head of the Optimus humanoid robot program is set to leave the firm. Tesla gave a June 22 date for the launch of Robotaxi in Austin. Any glow from a demo is unlikely to erase today’s headwinds. I don’t think it will be easy to turn around the recent sell-off, as Tesla’s EV business is in a critical stage.

Moreover, Tesla is trading at nosebleed levels with 145x expected earnings, whereas its peers are trading at decent valuations, signaling to avoid Tesla now. Due to these reasons, including weak demand, I downgrade Tesla to "Sell."

Trump and Musk's Conflict Overview

Recently, the Trump–Musk relationship turned openly hostile. Elon Musk publicly denounced President Trump’s proposed tax-cut bill on X, calling it a “disgusting abomination.” Trump said he’s “very disappointed” in Musk for opposing what he termed the “biggest tax cuts in history,” arguing it would add trillions to the deficit and roll back EV incentives. On Truth Social, Trump called Musk “crazy” and suggested reviewing Musk’s federal subsidies and contracts, potentially cutting off support for Tesla and SpaceX (SPACE). Musk retaliated online, warning that new tariffs “will cause a recession in the second half of this year,” and distanced himself from politics, exiting his Trump administration role in late May to focus more on Tesla and SpaceX.

Just a day ago, Elon Musk publicly apologized to Donald Trump, and Trump accepted the apology, causing a temporary policy ceasefire and a slight recovery in Tesla stock. This reconciliation lowers the policy overhang in the near term and eases some investor concerns about subsidy and contract risk. However, the political risk has not disappeared; the immediate danger of losing EV incentives or having government contracts canceled has subsided somewhat, but long-term policy risk remains until actual legislation shields EV incentives.

A key dispute centers on EV incentives: Trump removed tax credits, prompting criticism from Musk. Reports link this spat to Tesla’s stock swings, with shares plunging double digits at times and wiping over $100 billion in market value. Musk was once a major Trump backer (even the largest GOP donor in 2024), but investors now worry the feud may hurt Tesla. Facing criticism and falling Tesla sales, Musk has cut back on side projects like DOGE.

Investors cheered Musk’s comments that the DOGE project is “mostly done” and that he will refocus on Tesla, viewing this pivot away from crypto and politics as critical to restoring investor trust.

Tesla Delivery Numbers and Competitor Comparison

Tesla deliveries have seen a huge decline in 2025. In Q1 2025, the company reported about 336,681 global vehicle deliveries, a 13% down. China accounted for roughly 40% of that (134,607 units), reflecting Tesla’s second-largest market.

However, Tesla is now trailing its domestic rival. In April 2025, Chinese maker BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) sold 7,231 pure BEVs in Europe, slightly more than Tesla’s 7,165 in the same month. Tesla’s market share in Europe has been declining, as its lineup has aged and lower-priced Chinese models have arrived.

In fact, Tesla’s sales are down sharply in many European countries. A Reuters survey of May 2025 data shows Tesla registrations plunging 30–60% y/y in markets like Sweden, Portugal, and France, even as overall EV registrations are rising.

Norway was a notable bright spot; the refreshed Model Y launch boosted sales there, but otherwise, Tesla’s decline is industry-wide.

By contrast, BYD’s output is surging. BYD reported about 986,098 NEV (battery + plug-in) sales in Q1 2025, including 416,388 BEVs. That gave BYD a ~15.4% share of global BEV sales vs Tesla’s ~12.6%. Other Chinese automakers (SAIC, Geely, etc.) and even startups like Xiaomi are also eating into Tesla’s China and Europe business.

Meanwhile, in the U.S. market, Tesla still leads EV sales by a wide margin, but competition is emerging. Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), for example, delivered only 8,640 vehicles in Q1 2025. Rivian's Q1 Sales Fell 36% Amid L.A. Wildfire Fallout (down 36% y/y), illustrating the gap between Tesla and smaller players. Tesla has promised new, affordable models and aims to stay #1 worldwide. But current delivery trends show Tesla holding a smaller share of the European and Chinese markets, while BYD, other Chinese brands, and even incumbents like VW gain ground.

Put simply, Tesla’s Q1 output of 337k remains large, but its growth is outpaced by BYD’s 1 million NEVs, and even Tesla’s China sales are flagging. May sales of China-made Model 3/Y fell 15% y/y to 61,662 units. These figures highlight Tesla’s leadership but also its eroding dominance as competition and market shifts intensify.

Tesla’s Profit Struggles and Financial Health

Tesla’s profitability has been under strain. In Q1 2025, Tesla’s automotive revenue fell about 20% year-on-year, and net income plunged 71% compared to Q1 2024.

Automotive gross profit was roughly $1.46 billion, yielding a gross margin of only 11.3%, well below the 15–20% levels of prior years.

Operating margin was near 2.1%, down 3.4 percentage points from a year earlier. These results missed analyst targets and underscored weak demand.

In its Q1 earnings, the company noted that margins were “stronger-than-expected” due to cost cuts, but revenue was still down, and profit collapsed.

Deutsche Bank’s analysts have warned Tesla’s automotive margins have sunk to the lowest on record as it ramps up sales incentives to boost volume. TSLA Q1 Earnings Miss Mark, Investors Pin Hopes on Robotaxi, Cheap EVs. Tesla's affordable EV, robotaxis in focus after Musk backlash, competition hurt demand.

One key headwind for Tesla is the U.S. tax credit policy. New 2024 rules tightened domestic content requirements, causing some Tesla models to lose the $7,500 federal EV tax credit. For example, the Cybertruck AWD and many Model 3/Y units built with non-U.S. batteries became ineligible. But under the 2025 requirements, vehicles must be assembled in North America and have at least 60% of battery components and critical minerals sourced from qualifying U.S. or free-trade countries to claim the full credit. This effectively raised Tesla’s effective prices and may have shaved demand.

J.P. Morgan analysts estimate that the elimination of these credits (and related California incentives) could cut Tesla’s annual profit by roughly $1.2 billion.

Moreover, Trump’s “One Big Beautiful Bill” (and follow-on proposals) would remove all remaining EV incentives, a move that Musk explicitly blamed for their fallout. In short, reduced subsidies mean thinner profit margins on each car and lower volume, pressuring Tesla’s finances.

Finally, Tesla’s balance sheet shows some stress. Tesla’s debt-to-equity ratio was about 0.66, reflecting moderate leverage (debt $49.8 billion vs. equity $75.4 billion).

Tesla’s free cash flow narrowed significantly in Q1 2025, falling to about $664 million from nearly $2.8 billion in Q4 2024. The decline comes as the company ramps up investments in new factories and products.

Put together, the low margins, disappearing subsidies, modest net cash flow, and rising tariffs have sapped Tesla’s profitability. These financial indicators, steep profit declines, lower margins, credit losses, and weaker cash flow, highlight the financial stress Tesla faced in 2025 and why its investors have grown uneasy about its near-term outlook.

Valuation Analysis

While looking at Tesla's valuation, the multiples look extreme. Its forward P/E is 145× and P/S is 15×, dwarfing peers.

BYD, by comparison, trades around 20 to 25× forward P/E and 1.4× P/S. The broader auto and EV sector is much cheaper. For example, the auto industry's median P/S is around 3 times and P/E is at 16.7 times. Even luxury automakers typically trade below 5×. In short, Tesla’s valuation is roughly 3× higher on a revenue basis than a typical EV maker.

Compared to another peer, Toyota Motor Corporation (TM), which trades at a 0.70 Forward sales ratio, indicates that the company is significantly undervalued compared to Tesla and also the sector median of 0.90x.

Analysts expect Tesla to grow sales by about 17 to 18% in 2025, reaching approximately $117 billion. At the current stock price of around $300, a 155× P/E implies only $2 EPS next year, meaning Tesla’s valuation already assumes massive profit growth. To justify a 9.8× P/S ratio (compared to the 3× sector median), Tesla would need to roughly triple its earnings, or the stock would need to fall by about two-thirds.

For instance, increasing non-GAAP EPS from $2 to $6 would bring the forward P/E closer to 50× and the P/S ratio in line with peers.

Even with generous assumptions, Tesla’s fair value is much lower than current levels. A 50× multiple on $3 to $4 EPS points to a fair share price of around $150. Using a 3× P/S on $117 billion in revenue suggests a market cap of about $350 billion, or $100 per share. This implies Tesla is significantly overvalued unless it delivers much faster earnings growth.

Risks to My Thesis

Bulls often say the game is not yet over for Tesla as it has some catalysts in the face of Robotaxi and Optimus. Tesla has signaled a mid-2025 launch for its autonomous Robotaxi service in Austin (recent reports target June 22, 2025, with volume production of the purpose-built Cybercab slated for 2026. Realised, the Robotaxi fleet would open a large new ride-hailing revenue stream and reinforce Tesla’s lead in full autonomy. The Cybercab’s minimalist “unboxed” design also promises sharply lower production costs, potentially boosting margins on high-utilisation vehicles.

Tesla’s Optimus humanoid robot is on a slower track. The Q1 2025 shareholder deck states Optimus “builds on our Fremont pilot line in 2025.” Musk has since indicated low-volume production for factory use in 2025, scaling up (and selling externally) by 2026.

Strategically, deploying Optimus in Tesla factories could cut labor costs and improve efficiency in handling repetitive tasks in logistics/manufacturing while positioning Tesla in a nascent robotics market. Over time, Optimus sales might also diversify Tesla’s business, but material revenue is likely beyond the next year.

Conclusion

For me, Tesla is in a critical situation where it's currently facing plenty of challenges, including political headwinds and financial issues. However, the success of robotaxi could act as a catalyst for the company, but it will not be easy, as Tesla must navigate complex regulations, prove safety and reliability, and scale the service amid fierce competition.

The recent Musk–Tesla reconciliation provides a short-lived policy reprieve, easing some subsidy and contract fears, but underlying demand challenges and stretched multiples persist. At about 145× forward earnings, Tesla’s valuation reflects sky-high expectations; there’s virtually no room for error without risking a steep correction.

Any misstep, slower deliveries, margin misses, or delays in robotaxi/Optimus could spark a significant downside. I see little upside from here over the next few months and maintain a Sell recommendation until more tangible catalysts emerge.

data.title: Red Alert: Beware False Dividend Stocks
data.description:
Dividend stocks are not the safe haven that investors think. Anyone thinking that dividend stocks are a good strategy to succeed in these turbulent markets needs to think twice and read on.

We recently hosted a live training session warning investors about the dangers of fake dividend stocks. Fake Dividend Stocks might pay a nice dividend, but the risk of a significant decline in stock price more than offsets any potential gains investors might expect from the dividend. In other words, if a stock pays a 5% dividend but drops 20%, then investors lose a lot of money on that stock despite the 5% dividend.

In this report, we’re here to warn you of another type of bad dividend stock:

False Dividend Stocks

False Dividend Stocks pay a dividend, but the company doesn’t generate enough cash flow to afford the dividend. Therefore, the high risk of seeing the dividend cut along with the likely drop in the stock price due to the dividend cut more than offsets the potential gains investors might expect from a False Dividend Stock.

Successful dividend investing is not about finding companies that pay dividends. It’s about finding companies that can afford to keep paying and, hopefully, grow their dividend. A dividend is only good if the company generates the cash flows to afford it.

You don’t want your “income stock” to stop paying dividends because the company can’t afford it. Investors need to know if past dividends have been funded by taking on more debt or spending down cash balances.

The only way to avoid False Dividend Stocks is with diligent fundamental research on cash flows. As we’ve proven many times, investors must do their homework to get the truth about cash flow.

Below, we break down just how prevalent False Dividend Stocks are in the current market.

Pinpointing the False Dividend Risk

To find False Dividend Stocks, we first screen our entire coverage universe for stocks that pay a dividend. Of course, if a stock does not pay a dividend, it cannot be a False Dividend Stock.

Of the ~3,300 stocks under coverage, 1,416 stocks, or 43%, pay a dividend. Only 44 stocks, or 1% of the universe, qualify as Good Dividend Stocks because they earn a spot in one of our dividend model portfolios: Safest Dividend Yield Stocks and Dividend Growth Stocks.

Figure 1: Dividend Paying Stocks in Coverage Universe – As of June 4, 2025

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

After we’ve identified the dividend stock-paying universe, we then parse out the False Dividend Stocks, i.e., companies with negative free cash flow (FCF) in the trailing twelve-month (TTM) period.

Per Figure 2, there are 344 dividend-paying stocks with negative FCF in the TTM period as of June 4, 2025.

Figure 2: Dividend Stocks with Negative TTM FCF – As of June 4, 2025

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

The False Dividend Stocks

Below, we reveal three of the 344 riskiest False Dividend Stocks. These stocks earn our worst rating, Very Unattractive. Not only are the dividends false, but the stocks are also very expensive and overvalued.

Worse yet, there’s a higher risk that these three companies will have to cut their dividend because their dividend payments exceed cash flows not just in the TTM but over the past five years. In other words, these companies have been burning cash to pay dividends for an extended period.

These companies are digging themselves quite a hole here. See the last column in Figure 3, the Dividend Deficit as a % of TTM Revenue. This shows how much dividend payments have exceeded cash flows over the past five years as a percent of revenue. This metric shows how high the risk that these companies will have to cut their dividend is because they do not have the revenues, much less the income, to fund dividends.

Figure 3: Three Very Unattractive Rated False Dividend Stocks

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

CTO Realty Growth (CTO): 8.3% Dividend Yield

CTO Realty Growth earns its spot on the False Dividend Stocks list with -$189 million in FCF over the TTM period. CTO Realty Growth’s Dividend Deficit (FCF minus Dividends) as a % of TTM Revenue is -438% as dividends paid ($160 million) exceeded FCF (-$419 million) by $579 million over the last 5 years. From 2020-Q1'25, CTO Realty Growth’s cumulative Dividend Deficit is -$661 million. See Figure 4.

Figure 4: CTO Realty Growth’s Dividend Deficit: FCF Minus Dividends: 2020-Q1'25

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

The shareholder dilution required to fund this huge dividend deficit should make management blush.

Specifically, CTO Realty Growth’s total debt increased 116% from $273 million in 2020 to $603 million in the TTM ended Q1'25. The company’s shares outstanding increased 86% from 17.7 million to 32.9 million over the same time.

Making matters worse, CTO Realty Growth earns a low return on invested capital (ROIC) of just 1% and has a 2-year average free cash flow (FCF) yield of -10%. CTO’s price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio is -1.4 due to its negative economic book value, or no growth value, of -$13/share. Furthermore, in the default scenario of our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model, the CTO has a market-implied Growth Appreciation Period (GAP) of greater than 100 years. Poor fundamentals and an expensive stock price earn CTO a Very Unattractive Stock Rating.

The AES Corp (AES): 6.9% Dividend Yield

The AES Corp is also a False Dividend Stock due to its negative $719 million in FCF over the TTM. AES Corp’s Dividend Deficit as a % of TTM Revenue is -88% as dividends paid ($2.1 billion) exceeded FCF (-$8.5 billion) by $10.6 billion over the last 5 years. From 2020-Q1'25, AES Corp’s cumulative Dividend Deficit is -$11.5 billion. See Figure 5.

Figure 5: AES Corp’s Dividend Deficit: FCF Minus Dividends: 2020-Q1'25

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Similar to CTO Realty Growth, the shareholder dilution required to fund this huge dividend deficit is scary. The AES Corp’s total debt increased 57% from $20.3 billion in 2020 to $31.9 billion in the TTM ended Q1'25. The company’s shares outstanding increased 7% from 665.4 million to 711.9 million over the same time.

The AES Corp also generates an ROIC of 2% and a 2-yr Avg FCF yield of -4%. The AES Corp’s PEBV ratio is -0.7 with an economic book value of -$14/share. Furthermore, in the default scenario of our reverse DCF model, AES has a market-implied GAP greater than 100 years. Poor fundamentals and an expensive stock price earn AES a Very Unattractive Stock Rating.

Edison International (EIX): 5.9% Dividend Yield

Edison International burned $1.1 billion in FCF over the TTM, and the cash burn has been even worse over the past five years. Edison International’s Dividend Deficit as a % of TTM Revenue is -60% as dividends paid ($5.6 billion) exceeded FCF (-$4.7 billion) by $10.4 billion over the last 5 years. From 2020-Q1'25, Edison International’s cumulative Dividend Deficit is -$12.1 billion. See Figure 6.

Figure 6: Edison International’s Dividend Deficit: FCF Minus Dividends: 2020-Q1'25

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

As with the two stocks above, investors should note the shareholder dilution required to fund this huge dividend deficit. Total debt increased 35% from $37.3 billion in 2020 to $50.4 billion in the TTM ended Q1'25. The company’s shares outstanding increased by 2% from 378.9 million to 384.8 million over the same time.

Edison International earns a bottom quantile ROIC of just 3% while its 2-year average FCF Yield sits at -4%. Edison International’s PEBV ratio is 3.5, with an economic book value of just $15/share. In the default scenario of our reverse DCF model, EIX has a market-implied GAP of 5 years, which indicates the stock may not be as overvalued as the two above. However, the company’s poor fundamentals and high PEBV ratio still earn EIX a Very Unattractive Stock Rating.

data.title: UroGen Pharma: Lessons Learned And Future Outlook
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

My recent coverage of UroGen Pharma (NASDAQ:URGN) was the prospective risk-taker's manifesto on a very shaky FDA decision. Now that this thesis has worked out, the "predictions" laid out in that article appear to be nearly prophetic. I have been asked to revisit this stock, post approval, to see what we've learned, as well as to update the thesis now that the near-term existential threat to URGN has been dissolved.

UroGen Pharma Pipeline Updates

The obvious milestone event for URGN has been the approval of a formulation of mitomycin for the treatment of recurrent, low-grade, intermediate-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer, offering an alternative, non-surgical local treatment for this form of cancer.

This follows an interesting saga where URGN began a randomized trial for the drug, and then terminated that study in favor of a non-controlled study that would serve as the foundation for their approval submission. This goes against the FDA's advice (disclosed in ODAC proceedings) to conduct a randomized, controlled trial.

At the end of the day, the ODAC had a negative outcome for URGN, voting 5-4 against recommending approval. The unusual fact that 3 of the 4 "yes" votes came from urologists (joined by breast oncologist Bill Gradishar) gave hope that the FDA might execute the rare move of ignoring the vote of the ODAC's decision, although it's worth pointing out that the FDA considered this a "split" vote from what I've heard, and not an outright negative result.

Lesson #1: Sensible contrarian views can pay off

It remains clear that the FDA moves independently of the ODAC, even in today's day and age where priorities and strategies are in flux. There are clearly times where you can take the contrarian stance against the headwinds and come out the better for it. At the time of writing, URGN stock has surged from a low below $4 per share to over $11 per share in less than a month. This despite the running commentary I saw on social media that the company has "only a 11.5% chance" of getting approved, based on the number of times the FDA and ODAC have disagreed.

If you look deeper and seek to understand the context and nuance, these opportunities have a way of presenting themselves. This was never really an 11.5% chance of success, if you consider the mitigating factors.

Lesson #2: Don't get overconfident

However, one paying attention to this saga should take deep lessons from the rollercoaster ride that it's been. Just because that last article "predicted" the outcome does not mean that taking a contrarian stance on a beatdown biotech is always going to be the right option. Very smart, talented analysts disagreed with me on the likelihood of approval in that article, and they also disagreed with me on the downside risk being more or less priced in.

I do not think they were wrong. I'm letting it serve as a reminder of the capriciousness of biotech investing and trading. A school of investing thought best embodied on Seeking Alpha by Jonathan Faison is to right-size your positions relative to the risk involved. And a stock like URGN has the danger of making someone overconfident about their ability to pick the winners among the headwinds.

With this FDA approval, now all attention will turn to how well URGN is able to market their drug. And the key questions that now remain relate to physician uptake, given that the surgery in question that it would replace is well entrenched in the standard of care.

URGN Stock Financial Overview

URGN's current financial situation has not meaningfully changed since my last coverage of the company back in May. To summarize, the company had about $196 million in total liquidity at the end of Q1 2025, as well as $122 million in long-term debt. Their cash runway stood at around 4 to 5 quarters, based on a quarterly cash burn rate of $42 million.

At the time of writing, URGN has not announced any kind of public offering or other funding mechanism to support the initial commercialization of UGN-102 post-approval. It is worth noting that they will be turning over a percentage of revenue as royalty to RTW, based on a 2021 agreement that provided URGN with a lump sum of cash to support development and initial commercialization.

As for the market, in addition to Jelmyto, which is already marketed by URGN and saw $20.2 million in revenues in Q1 2025 ($17.9 million gross profit), URGN anticipates that for UGN-102, it estimates that there are around 59,000 patients with low-grade, recurrent NMIBC who would currently be managed with surgery alone. The company sees a potential $5 billion market for the low-grade NMIBC setting, per their corporate presentation.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - An existential risk has been eliminated

The strong move from the stock price's nadir reflects the market's pessimism about the FDA's decision. Contrast the price movement with that of Nuvation Bio's first drug approval (currently down some 34% from pre-approval prices), and you can really see how the element of surprise factors into market valuations.

Before this approval was locked in, URGN stood at the precipice of oblivion. Without approval, their debt load was high, and they faced a massive obligation to RTW to repay that royalty agreement. Now they have forward momentum to see how much of the market they can capture.

Risk - It remains to be seen how successful UGN-102 will be

As I stated above, URGN sees an opportunity here in the multiples of billions. The reality may end up being a bit more disappointing once rubber hits the road. They tout in their corporate materials and in their ODAC case how favorably UGN-102 is viewed by urologists. However, when it comes down to it, we may see resistance to uptake of a new, expensive drug when TURBT offers disease control and has a long history.

The dynamics of market penetration are going to need to be watched closely.

Risk - Liquidity vs debt in the early commercialization stage

Assuming the drug launch goes well, there is still the matter of cash burn that URGN is facing. They are sitting on around a year's worth of funding, more or less, and that may not be enough to reach a break-even point, assuming that ever comes. All the more reason to watch the initial launch metrics closely. Moreover, the RTW arrangement will take a large chunk of the revenues in the form of royalties, being entitled to 9.5%, 3.0%, and 1.0% of annual net sales up to $200, $300, and above $300 million, respectively.

Bottom-Line Summary

There is a lot to learn from watching how the URGN story has developed in 2025, and I think, rather than jumping in with both feet now, it is a valuable lesson in how to right-size the biotech stocks in your portfolio and manage risk.

As for the URGN investment thesis, at these price levels it starts to become a little murky as we await the drug launch. We just don't know yet how well the sales will be out of the gate, and URGN does not have a comfortable amount of cash runway to navigate bumps. In my opinion, it would not be surprising to see the company conduct a secondary offering, although transactions with companies like RTW suggest that they would prioritize non-dilutive financing.

For now, I am maintaining a "Buy" sentiment here, but it isn't quite as strong as in my first coverage of the company, even though so much risk has been removed. Now, URGN will live and die by its sales execution. They have experience in this with Jelmyto, but UGN-102 will be a different beast. Regardless, URGN is going to be a stock to watch closely for the rest of 2025.

data.title: Yellow Pages Limited: Still A 'Yield Trap,' But Catalysts Could Emerge
data.description:
Yellow Pages Limited (OTCPK:YLWDF) may be well-known among Canadian-based investors as a high-yield dividend stock, but investors around the world may be curious as to whether this legacy phonebook publisher has tried making a SaaS-transformation, similar to that of its American counterpart, Thryv Holdings (THRY), has.

I've covered Thryv previously here on Seeking Alpha, arguing that, despite it taking longer than expected, within two or three years, that company's CRM software pivot could finally pay off, resulting in THRY stock doubling or tripling from present price levels.

So, is there a similar setup at play with YLWDF stock? Not exactly. Although Yellow Pages generates 80% of its annual revenue from digital sources, this digital business is more akin to other types of online platforms generating revenue from advertising rather than from steady subscription sales.

Although there are some possible catalysts that could play out in the years ahead, it is far too speculative to say that YLWDF is on the cusp of shaking off its "value trap"/"yield trap" status.

Yellow Pages: Background

Much like the corporate predecessors to Thryv, Yellow Pages' corporate predecessors consist of the yellow pages businesses of Canadian incumbent local exchange carriers BCE (BCE) and Telus Corporation (TU). The company's full history goes back over a century, but what's most relevant to investors today is the company's history since its transformation into primarily a digital advertising company starting in the early 2010s.

At first, this transformation, which occurred at the same time that Yellow Pages had to execute a highly-dilutive debt-for-equity restructuring to avoid bankruptcy, was well received by the market. Within two years of this restructuring, shares were up more than threefold.

However, the success of this transformation proved fleeting. In the mid-to-late 2010s, Yellow Pages' digital revenue peaked, then also began to decline as well. This resulted in a massive drop for YLWDF stock during this time frame.

Revenue and earnings have continued to fall, but as seen in the chart above, YLWDF shares remain up from their multi-year low set in 2017. From the late 2010s until 2021, shares were even in the midst of making a major comeback.

It's not surprising that this stock experienced a renewal in bullishness during this time. For one, the company at that time was successfully slowing down the rate of overall revenue decline, referred to by management as the "bending of the revenue curve," and a sign of progress towards stabilized revenue and earnings.

Also, after several years of forgoing dividends and buybacks, in favor of debt repayment, by 2020, a much-less levered Yellow Pages reinstated its quarterly dividend. The company also began to aggressively repurchase shares.

However, as inflationary pressures and a push to stabilize digital sales began to keep operating expenses steady while revenue continued to decline, albeit at a slower pace, shares again came under pressure. Add in the impact of higher interest rates, which typically have an inverse relation to high-yield dividend stock prices, YLWDF has given back a good portion of its post-pandemic gains.

Recent Developments

On May 14, Yellow Pages Limited released results for the quarter ending March 31, 2025. As before, CEO David Eckert touted the company "bending of the curve," noting that the rate of overall revenue decline improved compared to the preceding quarter.

Eckert also talked up the company's adjusted EBITDA margins and strong cash balance. However, while it's fine for management to put a positive spin on YLWDF's fiscal performance, looking at the numbers without taking into account this commentary, one can hardly feel confident about the future direction of Yellow Pages Limited.

During the quarter, the company reported $50.8 million CAD ($37.3 million) in revenue, down 7.6% from the prior year's quarter. Despite the talking up about EBITDA margins, these were still down from Q1 2024, falling from 27.8% to 23.4%. Net income fell by around 40.5%, from $8.4 million CAD ($6.2 million) to $5 million CAD ($3.67 million).

Admittedly, there is further context to these declines, besides the secular decline of YLWDF's legacy business, as well as the continued lackluster performance of the company's digital segment. Yellow Pages is in the midst of turning around the digital segment, through the build out of its advertising sales force, as discussed on the latest post-earnings conference call.

However, it's not as if this is a newly-implemented turnaround strategy. As hinted above, Yellow Pages has been engaging in this sales force expansion for years. Take a look at this post-earnings conference call from two years ago, and David Eckert is making statements about it similar to those he just made last month.

While laudable that management is working to turn around the business, after several years of the same initiative, but with revenue and earnings continuing to decline, it is highly doubtful that this is a viable strategy.

YLWDF Stock Valuation

While YLWDF shares have gained by 25% over the past twelve months, the stock remains far below its multiyear lows. Clearly, the market has already decided that the ongoing turnaround game plan is not worth factoring into the company's valuation.

At current prices, YLWDF stock is valued as one would expect a steadily-declining business to be valued. That is, shares sell at a valuation of just 7.4 times trailing twelve month (TTM) earnings, and at a TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of 3.9x.

To some degree, it's difficult to find highly relevant publicly-traded peers to Yellow Pages Limited. While in some ways the Canadian version of Thryv Holdings, a lack of a growing segment, such as Thryv's eponymous SaaS-based platform, makes it difficult to say that YLWDF's valuation should come anywhere close to that of THRY.

Yes, there are some publicly-traded companies that have businesses similar to that of Yellow Pages' digital segment. Names like Angie's List parent Angi (ANGI), as well as Yelp (YELP) come to mind.

While both of these companies are facing growth headwinds of their own right now, as neither companies is experiencing the same sort of steady profitability decline, I don't see YLWDF re-rating to a valuation on par with ANGI and YELP, which trade for around 10x non-GAAP earnings, anytime soon.

That said, there may be a path for YLWDF to gain in price, but only if the company abandons its current game plan, and pivots towards a "runoff" type strategy for not just the print business, but the digital business as well. In other words, a focus on aggressively reducing operating costs at the digital segment, maximizing cash flow, even if the rate of revenue/earnings decline picks up in speed.

Several Catalysts That Could Emerge From Left Field

Given Yellow Pages' high hedge fund ownership, including significant ownership from investors who have been involved with the company since its early-2010s recapitalization, it's surprising that they have stuck with the stabilization strategy.

Considering the success of firms Alden Global implementing a run-off strategy in industries like newspapers, as well as the current low valuation of YLWDF stock, it may make sense for these entities to take the company private, maximizing cash flow from this slowly-melting but still-melting ice cube. If these firms aren't interested in doing this themselves, perhaps eventually they will seek out another financial buyer to acquire this company.

That said, I wouldn't buy YLWDF stock, merely on the potential for it to be acquired or to go private. Consider this a long-shot catalyst. Fortunately, there are a few more possible catalysts that could emerge from left field that may arguably have a greater possibility of happening.

For instance, since May 2024, the Bank of Canada, like other central banks around the world, has been cutting interest rates, after raising them in 2022/2023 due to rising inflation. Bank of Canada in fact has been cutting rates more aggressively than the U.S. Federal Reserve, lowering them from 5% a year ago to 2.7% today, versus the Fed's 100-basis point reduction from 5.5% to 4.5% since last fall.

This has likely been a factor in YLWDF's rise in price over the past twelve months. If Canadian interest rates fall further, perhaps we will see an additional positive re-rating of shares, given how high-yield dividend stocks typically rise in tandem with falling interest rates.

Further increases to YLWDF's dividends could also help to re-rate the stock. With a current cash position of around $43.2 million CAD ($31.7 million USD), or over 20% of the company's market cap, Yellow Pages may be in a position to execute a large share repurchase once again.

Alongside return-of-capital efforts, there may be the potential for Yellow Pages to soon report results that are better, or at least "less bad," than the continued declines reported in the latest quarterly earnings release. The sales force ramp-up may still be in motion, but operating costs in other areas could move lower.

For example, YLWDF's legacy pension liabilities. A few weeks ago, Yellow Pages announced that it had transferred half of its long-term pension obligations to a third-party. While the company appears to still be on the hook to make additional payments to stabilize the plan, as it's done in the past, these payments could trend lower going forward.

The Bottom Line on YLWDF Stock

While the situation with Yellow Pages is anything but dire, it's not as if there is much of a compelling reason for investors, whether located in Canada, the U.S., or elsewhere, to consider this name worthy of a buy.

YLWDF stock may offer up a high forward dividend yield (9.17%), but a retreat back to prior lows, or down to even lower prices, could wipe out these steady gains. Barring a further reduction in interest rates, the market isn't likely to re-rate this stock, unless the company finally starts to report improved results.

Add in the questionable prospect of other possible catalysts, such as the potential for Yellow Pages to be taken private or sold to a private equity buyer, and the verdict here is clear. You may want to keep an YLWDF stock, in case the situation changes, but otherwise seek better value and yield opportunities elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oracle: A Textbook Example Of Recency Bias
data.description:
Following yet another strong quarter, Oracle Corporation's (NYSE:ORCL) shares have skyrocketed by more than 13% on the day and with that, the stock now sits at an all-time high and the co-founder Larry Ellison is now the second-richest person - after his close friend Elon Musk.

The sentence written above would have been characterized as fiction just a few years ago when sentiment around ORCL was at a record low and the stock was trading at $55 a piece.

A few things make ORCL a unique case of emotional biases in investing and a perfect example of how investors could outperform the market by going against predominant narratives.

  • Firstly, Oracle's success in the cloud space was not something that happened overnight, and the signs were plain to see a couple of years ago.
  • Secondly, ORCL is one of the largest publicly traded enterprises, and it clearly illustrates that the market could have the wrong expectations even for the largest companies in the world.
  • Lastly, it appears that the sentiment pendulum has now swung in the opposite direction and this is a major red flag for anyone buying ORCL at current levels.

From An Underdog To A Leader

Before we evaluate just how sustainable is the recent run in ORCL share price, we should take a closer look at the change in sentiment in recent years and to what extent is this supported by actual business performance.

In early 2021, I outlined why Oracle's stock deserves more attention. In addition to the conservative valuation, the analysis focused on the significant progress being made not only in the Software-as-a-Service space (where the company's main competitive advantages were), but also in the cloud infrastructure space - an area that was highly complementary to the existing service offering.

If you follow the link above, you will see that at the time ORCL was ranked as a niche player with low ability to execute in the Cloud Infrastructure and Platform services. In the analysis, however, I showed that Oracle's cloud (OCI) was already moving in the right direction. On top of that, just recently, the co-founder Larry Ellison made some comments that the OCI has become capacity-constrained by the unusually high demand.

In hindsight, all that was sufficient for investors to become very optimistic about ORCL's strategic positioning in the cloud infrastructure space. However, as most investors remained focused on the past and on the current standing of the OCI, they were unable to see where the company was headed in the coming years. Now, just a few years later, Oracle is already ranked among the leaders in the space and the gap with AWS, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is likely to continue to narrow.

Perhaps what did come as a surprise was the massive AI-induced demand which has accelerated demand for ORCL's applications and infrastructure services.

No other company is even attempting to build the depth and breadth of AI-based applications that we have already built. Oracle will build more cloud infrastructure data centers than all of our infrastructure competitors combined. All of our OCI data centers from the smallest low-cost data center to the largest gigawatt AI training data center include all Oracle OCI capabilities. A large percentage of those capabilities are autonomous.

Source: Oracle Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript.

To illustrate the scale of this unprecedented boom in demand, we could take a closer look at ORCL's capital expenditure to revenue on a quarterly basis. For comparison, the jump in this ratio in FY 2023 was quite high. In recent quarters, however, Oracle has begun spending roughly half of its quarterly sales on capex to meet future demand.

This is also indicative of demand for Oracle's application suite and its database as these services benefit from the cloud's expansion.

This intertwining between IaaS and SaaS services demand is something that I talked about in my first analyses on Oracle (see the links provided above). They also create a major competitive advantage for the company over pure SaaS players.

It seems, however, that many investors are grappling with this reality only recently as the acceleration of cloud infrastructure revenue growth in the last quarter also helped Oracle's cloud application segment and the ERP suites in particular.

For comparison, a year ago, the two ERP suites grew at 14% and 19% respectively, while these growth rates accelerated to nearly 20% during the last quarter of FY 2025 (see above).

Earlier this week, it also became clear that ORCL's management now expects the cloud revenue to grow over 70% during the current year. Thus, total revenue growth is anticipated to be around 16%.

OCI has seen exceptional demand for infrastructure services and those contracted noncancelable bookings in RPO give us confidence that OCI revenue will grow over 70% this current year.

(...) we are raising our revenue guidance for fiscal year '26 to over $67 billion, up 16% for the year.

Source: ORCL Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript.

This figure has caught analysts off-guard as the current forward revenue growth rate is slightly below that.

I have been warning that investors should not fall victim to recency bias for quite some time, and it now appears that investors who listened have been rewarded.

However, the impact of investors' emotional biases goes both ways. While it could result in a share price being significantly undervalued, it could just as easily swing the valuation in the other direction.

Is Optimism Now Going Too Far?

Over the past year alone, ORCL's share price appreciated by more than 40%. What should be noted, however, is that the stock experienced significant volatility during this period. As we can see on the graph below, ORCL was down almost 15% in April of this year, just to skyrocket over the past month or so.

Such wild swings are worth noting by investors, especially at times when the equity market is at nose-bleed valuations. Moreover, the sharp increase in ORCL share price in recent months was not accompanied by a similar increase in trading volumes and that brought the stock to a price/sales ratio of nearly 10 - a multi-year high.

The consensus rating of Wall Street analysts is also at its highest levels and soon enough the ORCL trade could become extremely one-sided with all analysts being bullish on the stock.

In addition to everything said above, Oracle is also priced at extremely high levels on a normalized free cash flow basis (more on that later).

The current investment cycle has now resulted in a negative free cash flow - definitely good news for investors, but also a double-edged sword as a potential downturn in the economy could make investors nervous about Oracle's all-in approach.

The capex increase is truly unprecedented with the ratio of Capex to Depreciation expense now standing at above 500%.

To see what the normalized free cash flow yield for ORCL is, we will assume that the capex-to-depreciation expense ratio would return to 100%. But even if this were to happen today, then ORCL's free cash flow yield would stand at 3% - near a record low, despite the significant increase in cash flow from operations in recent years.

While double-digit revenue growth is expected to sustain the share price in FY 2026, investors buying at current levels are increasingly exposed to the risk of growth assumptions beyond that. Therefore, while momentum in ORCL share price could be sustained in the near term (provided that the equity market does not turn south), the risk-reward profile of the stock is vastly different from the one when I first laid out my investment thesis in late 2020.

Investor Takeaway

The ORCL stock has become a textbook example of how recency bias in the equity market could create significant opportunities that are hiding in plain sight. Now that the market has finally realized the true potential of Oracle's long-term strategy in the cloud sector, the investment thesis for the company has changed dramatically. In my view, the stock fully reflects the ongoing improvements in business performance, and it now trades increasingly on momentum. This also means that in order for ORCL to continue to outperform the market, then the company should also continue to deliver better-than-expected returns. That's a much riskier endeavour for investors and even though ORCL could continue to perform well in the coming months, I am not willing to take this risk and I retain a Hold rating.

data.title: A Brief Lesson On Huge Yielders
data.description:
Let’s get educational! It’s like going to college but with more numbers and no parties. On the positive side, the price is right. Why educational articles? That’s too many questions.

We’re going to talk about AGNC Investment Corp (AGNC) because consensus EPS was around $1.62 for the year and that would be 19.6% on the Q1 2025 tangible book value of $8.25. That’s really high. If you’re not familiar with mortgage REITs, that might sound reasonable. It is not. Now we’re looking into the earnings:

You’ll notice the average asset yield has been increasing, but the rate of growth clearly declined. You’ll also notice that the average cost of funds has generally been trending higher. Not every single quarter, but most of the time. If interest rates remained relatively unchanged for several years, that would be great for agency mortgage REITs. They love it when interest rates don’t change. However, you would expect the average cost of funds to be trending above 4%. Why? Because they can’t just borrow at below-market rates forever.

Currently, they still have enough hedges in place to keep their interest expense low. But those hedges will be rolling off. You can verify that using this graphic:

About $9.25 billion of swaps have one year or left remaining to maturity. Further, the weighted average maturity of those swaps is only 0.3 years. That means those hedges are going to burn off rapidly.

Why does that matter so much? Because they are receiving 4.38% and paying 0.15%. In other words, the shortest-duration swaps currently have a spread of 4.23%.

Applied to the notional amount of $9.25 billion, those swaps would save them $391 million annually (if they were not expiring).

That’s a big deal. There are about 922 million common shares outstanding. Therefore, those swaps are enhancing net interest income by about $.42 per share annually. Is $.42 per share a big amount in the context of $1.62 per share for consensus earnings? Yes.

So, if you were wondering why AGNC’s earnings would decline, congratulations. You understand.

This isn’t a new problem. This has been coming for years. It’s a major reason that the net interest spread for AGNC was declining. It used to be over 3%. Now it’s averaging around 2%. Don’t take my word for it. It was in the first graphic I posted (bottom-left corner).

This is part of why earning over 19% on book value isn’t sustainable.

Does this mean AGNC is dead? No. Does it mean there’s a big problem with their preferred shares? Absolutely not. But it does mean investors who think they completed due diligence by looking at consensus EPS aren’t the sharpest tools in the shed. To be fair, pretty much everything in the universe means that.

What The Board of Directors Think

I’m not going to pretend that I can speak for the board of directors. That would be absurd. So inappropriate. I’m going to let their actions speak for them.

If you think AGNC is cheap while trading far over book value, you may want to consider the change in shares outstanding:

Now, you might recall that I mentioned 922 million shares outstanding. Good job. You’re right! Using the values from the end of Q4 2024 does miss out on all the shares, AGNC issued during Q1 2025.

Of course, choosing that the company should issue shares doesn’t necessarily mean that they weren’t cheap. Maybe management was buying up shares for their personal accounts.

Oh, nope. That didn’t happen. From June 2020 through June 2025 there have been 47 insider trades tracked by Insider Cow:

  • 45 sales
  • 2 purchases

Of course, it’s normal for management to get stock-based compensation and then dump the shares for cash.

Here’s a chart showing the trailing NAV (Net Asset Value, same as Book Value) and the share price:

Ideas Always Welcome

We got some great suggestions for charts in the comments of our prior article. I’m still going over possibilities for how we might add some of those charts. So long as I can get past the uploading, I can provide quite a few charts. In many cases, readers are asking for images (or tables) using data we either already have or can acquire quickly. So feel free to check out what we already have and make suggestions in the comments here.

Charts

You can see all the charts here.

It’s just one extra click, and it saves me a ton of time in uploading the article. Better yet, reader responses were overwhelmingly positive.

All The Stocks

The charts compare the following companies and their preferred shares or baby bonds:

  • BDCs: (CSWC), (BXSL), (TSLX), (OCSL), (GAIN), (TPVG), (FSK), (MAIN), (ARCC), (GBDC), (OBDC), (SLRC)
  • Commercial mREITs: (GPMT), (FBRT), (BXMT)
  • Residential Hybrid mREITs: (MITT), (CIM), (RC), (MFA), (EFC), (NYMT)
  • Residential Agency mREITs: (NLY), (AGNC), (CHMI), (DX), (TWO), (ARR), (ORC)
  • Residential Originator and Servicer mREITs: (RITM), (PMT)

Build a foundation for steady income with REITs

As demand for key real estate sectors increases and supply fails to keep pace, 2025 presents a prime opportunity to invest in REITs, Preferreds, and BDCs.

The REIT Forum offers a proven, transparent approach with actionable insights. Led by Colorado Wealth Management Fund (aka Michael VanLoon) and an expert team, subscribers receive precise trade alerts, portfolio tracking, and exclusive articles. Strategies from The REIT Forum have consistently outperformed sector indexes for nearly a decade.

Start your risk-free two-week trial today and capitalize on the power of real estate returns.
